children.name: Coracias
children.description: True rollers — stocky, crow-sized birds with brilliant blue-and-turquoise plumage, named for their spectacular aerial courtship displays in which they tumble and roll through the air. They are among the most colourful birds of the Old World, found in open country across Europe, Africa, and Asia.

children.name: Coracias garrulus
children.description: A dazzling turquoise-and-blue bird of open farmland in southern and eastern Europe. The male's courtship display is unforgettable — he climbs to height, then tumbles head-over-tail, rolling from side to side while calling loudly. Has declined sharply across Europe due to agricultural intensification. Classified as Near Threatened.

children.name: Coracias caudatus
children.description: One of Africa's most photographed birds. Lilac breast, turquoise belly, blue crown, and elongated outer tail feathers. Found across eastern and southern Africa in savanna and open woodland. Often seen perched conspicuously on treetops and utility poles.

children.name: Coracias abyssinicus
children.description: Found in the Sahel region from Senegal to Ethiopia. Very similar to the Lilac-breasted Roller but with longer tail streamers and a darker blue throat. Inhabits dry savanna and acacia woodland.

children.name: Coracias benghalensis
children.description: A common and conspicuous bird of South Asia. Blue and turquoise plumage with a warm brown back. Often seen on wires and posts along roadsides. Known for its tumbling display flight. The state bird of several Indian states.

children.name: Coracias naevius
children.description: A roller with rufous crown from sub-Saharan Africa.

children.name: Coracias temminckii
children.description: A roller with purple wings from Sulawesi.

children.name: Coracias caudatus
children.description: One of Africa's most photographed birds, with lilac breast and elongated tail streamers.

children.name: Coracias caudatus
children.description: One of Africa's most photographed birds.

children.name: Eurystomus
children.description: Broad-billed rollers — a small genus of forest rollers with wider, more hooked bills and more subdued plumage than the true rollers. They are crepuscular feeders, hawking insects at dusk like giant swallows. Found across Africa, Asia, and Australasia.

children.name: Eurystomus glaucurus
children.description: A warm brown and purple-blue roller found across sub-Saharan Africa and Madagascar. Stockier and shorter-tailed than the true rollers. Often seen hawking insects from a high perch at dusk, giving a harsh 'kak-kak' call.

children.name: Eurystomus orientalis
children.description: The only roller found in Australia and East Asia. Dark blue-green plumage with a distinctive pale silvery-blue patch on each wing (the 'dollar'). Hunts from exposed perches, making aerial sallies for large insects. Named for the dollar-sized wing patch.
